citation-preserving back-reference, freeing section space for distinct findings.

§-1.3 CONSOLIDATE-KEEP-ALL (never a drop / cap / thin): every recycled instance KEEPS its own
numeric citation marker(s) inline on the back-reference — no citation is ever dropped, and NO
citation is moved between sections (so the per-section-local citation numbering the downstream
``_remap_section_markers_to_global`` relies on stays valid). Repetition is consolidated, not
deleted; the finding's full prose remains in its richest section.

EXACT-RECYCLE ONLY — NOT a paraphrase-collapse (Codex diff-gate iter-1 P1 #2): equivalence is an
EXACT normalized-text match (NUMERIC-citation-stripped, whitespace-collapsed, lowercased,
trailing-punctuation-stripped). Two sentences that differ in ANY content — a year, a figure, an
entity, directionality, an extra clause — have DIFFERENT normalized text and are NEVER clustered.
Crucially, ONLY renderer-guaranteed numeric citation markers (``[12]``) are stripped from the
signature; NON-citation bracketed content — a bracketed entity/label such as ``[Alpha]`` — is
PRESERVED in the signature (Codex diff-gate iter-2 P1). So ``The model [Alpha] cut error 10% [1]``
and ``The model [Beta] cut error 10% [2]`` have DIFFERENT signatures and are never clustered:

MARKER-REQUIRED — never rewrites an honest gap disclosure (Fable diff-gate iter-1 P1): a unit is an
eligible finding ONLY if it carries at least one NUMERIC ``[N]`` citation marker. Every

LAYOUT-PRESERVING (Codex diff-gate iter-1 P1 #3): a recycled instance is replaced by an in-place
single-occurrence substring swap on the ORIGINAL ``verified_text`` — every other byte (markdown
``### slot`` sub-headings, blank lines, adjacent sentences) is preserved unchanged. The guard NEVER
re-splits + ``" ".join``-rejoins a body (which would flatten ``### Heading`` layout into inline
prose and corrupt contract-section slot headings). A unit whose text contains a markdown heading

*,
    max_recompose_cap: int | None = None,
    outcomes: Sequence[SectionOutcome] | None = None,
    required_titles: Sequence[str] | None = None,
    min_sections: int = 0,
) -> RevisionApplyResult:
    """Apply validated ops deterministically and return the new plan set + the recompose set.

    ``keep`` reuses the wave-1 section byte-identical (never in the recompose set).
    ``merge`` builds ONE new section from the UNION of the merged ev_ids (never text-glue).
    ``split`` / ``retitle`` / ``reassign`` / ``add`` recompose the affected section(s) only.
    Wholesale-invalid input (parse failed, or zero accepted ops) => original plans unchanged,
    empty recompose set (fail-open to wave-1). Over the ``max_recompose`` ceiling, highest-impact
    ops win (dropped/undersupplied sections first) and the rest are DEFERRED as disclosed no-ops.

    ``required_titles`` (item 13): when the run has a user-required section structure, only
    ``keep``/``reassign`` may run (they preserve the exact-N-in-order contract); ``merge``/
    ``split``/``add``/``retitle`` are DEFERRED as disclosed no-ops so the structure cannot break,
    and the assembled order stays the required order. ``None``/empty => no restriction (unchanged).

    Item 4: an op whose target title was already consumed by an earlier op (merged/split away, or
    retitled) is skipped as a disclosed no-op (never a ghost recompose title); retitled sections are
    resolved by their CURRENT title. Item 5 (apply half): a NEW title colliding with a live section
    is deferred. Item 12: an ``add`` with no evidence, or a ``reassign`` that empties a section, is
    marked ``undersupplied=True`` (the gap is disclosed, never faked).

    ``min_sections`` (RACE-FLOOR fix, 2026-07-12): the corpus-derived thematic-coverage FLOOR — the
    minimum number of live sections the outline must retain. A ``merge`` whose net reduction
    (``len(titles) - 1``) would drop the running live-section count below this floor is DEFERRED as a
    disclosed no-op (``reason_code=min_sections_floor``). ``split``/``add`` still raise the count and
    are never blocked; ``keep``/``retitle``/``reassign`` are count-neutral. This stops the multi-turn
    loop from silently collapsing distinct corpus themes into fewer, thinner sections (the observed
    RACE 0.4447 -> 0.3518 regression). ``0`` => no floor (byte-identical legacy)."""
    cap = max_recompose_cap if max_recompose_cap is not None else max_recompose()
    base = [_as_dict(p) for p in plans]
    by_title = {p["title"]: p for p in base}

    def _ci(s: Any) -> str:
        return str(s).strip().lower()

    # item 13: required-structure lock — restrict ops to keep/reassign when required_titles present.
    required_lock = bool([t for t in (required_titles or []) if str(t).strip()])

content.text:
**My hypothesis is wrong, and so is part of Fable's.** The menus are *not* mirror-dominated (595 rows → 527 distinct works, only ~11% mirrors). And critically, the evidence is **not** the binding constraint:

| section | distinct works available | refs actually cited |
|---|---|---|
| Introduction | 166 | 7 |
| Theoretical Foundations | 15 | 8 (10 sentences on [1]) |
| Occupational Exposure | 48 | 17 |
| Productivity Gains | 57 | 8 |
| Empirical Evidence | 40 | 5 |

Seven of ten sections **have ≥15 distinct works available** — rule #9 is *satisfiable* in most of them. The writer simply isn't using the breadth it already has; it cites 5–8 works from a menu of 40–166 and milks the most quotable one. So Fable's "binding constraint is distinct usable evidence per section" is **refuted for 7 of 10 sections** — the constraint is the writer's *selection behavior*, and an oversized menu is what drives the milking (the same menu-overload effect Rank8 proved).

That means **Rank9 (cap=30) is already the right experiment** — it's testing exactly this. Let me build the density instrument Fable specified, then read Rank9 against it.

input.content:
"""Insight-density instrument: distinct WORKS cited per 1000 words.

Fable's pre-registered falsifier for the depth levers. Word count alone cannot tell a
deeper report from a padded one -- a run can double its length by restating the same
finding against more sources. Density can: it only rises if the report brings in
genuinely new works.

WORKS, NOT BIBLIOGRAPHY ENTRIES. The bibliography over-counts: one paper occupies several
entries when the corpus holds mirror rows of it (Brynjolfsson/Li/Raymond took SIX slots in
rank7). Reporting entries as "distinct sources" is how a 73-work report gets sold as a
107-source one. Collapse mirrors first, three ways, in order:
  1. same_work_groups from the corpus (authoritative -- the corpus already knows).
  2. DOI, when present (authoritative across mirrors).
  3. normalized title (the fallback: lowercased, stripped of punctuation and an "(also
     mirrored)" suffix, truncated -- catches mirrors the corpus never grouped).
Only entries actually CITED in the prose count. An uncited bibliography entry is not a
source the report used; counting it would re-inflate exactly the number this exists to
deflate.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"\[(\d+)\]")
_PUNCT_RE = re.compile(r"[^\w\s]")
_WS_RE = re.compile(r"\s+")
_MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE = re.compile(r"\(also mirrored\)", re.IGNORECASE)


def _norm_title(title: str) -> str:
    t = _MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE.sub(" ", str(title or "")).lower()
    t = _PUNCT_RE.sub(" ", t)
    t = _WS_RE.sub(" ", t).strip()
    return t[:70]


def _entries(bib: object) -> list[dict]:
    if isinstance(bib, list):
        return [e for e in bib if isinstance(e, dict)]
    if isinstance(bib, dict):
        for key in ("entries", "bibliography", "items"):
            v = bib.get(key)
            if isinstance(v, list):
                return [e for e in v if isinstance(e, dict)]
    return []


def _work_key(entry: dict, work_of: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    ev = str(entry.get("evidence_id") or entry.get("ev_id") or "")
    if ev in work_of:
        return work_of[ev]
    doi = str(entry.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
    if doi:
        return f"doi:{doi}"
    title = _norm_title(entry.get("title") or "")
    if title:
        return f"t:{title}"
    return f"ev:{ev}"


def _same_work_map(corpus_path: Path) -> dict[str, str]:
    """ev_id -> work key, from the corpus's own same_work_groups.

    The groups arrive as DICTS with ``member_evidence_ids`` -- not bare id lists. A version
    of this that assumed bare lists would match nothing and silently report zero mirrors,
    which reads identical to a corpus that genuinely has none.
    """
    if not corpus_path.exists():
        return {}
    corpus = json.loads(corpus_path.read_text())
    groups = corpus.get("same_work_groups") if isinstance(corpus, dict) else None
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    for g in groups or []:
        if not isinstance(g, dict):
            continue
        members = g.get("member_evidence_ids") or []
        if not members:
            continue
        key = g.get("same_work_id") or g.get("canonical_index") or members[0]
        for m in members:
            out[str(m)] = f"w:{key}"
    return out


def density(run_dir: Path, corpus_path: Path) -> dict:
    report = (run_dir / "report.md").read_text()
    bib = json.loads((run_dir / "bibliography.json").read_text())
    entries = _entries(bib)
    work_of = _same_work_map(corpus_path)

    # Prose cites by NUMERIC MARKER ([12]), never by ev_id -- an ev_\d+ regex over report.md
    # matches nothing and silently reports zero. Markers are 1-indexed into the bibliography.
    cited_markers = {int(m) for m in _MARKER_RE.findall(report)}
    cited_entries = [e for i, e in enumerate(entries, start=1) if i in cited_markers]

    words = len(report.split())
    works = {_work_key(e, work_of) for e in cited_entries}
    per_1k = 1000.0 * len(works) / words if words else 0.0
    return {
        "run": run_dir.name,
        "words": words,
        "bib_entries": len(entries),
        "cited_entries": len(cited_entries),
        "distinct_works": len(works),
        "works_per_1k_words": round(per_1k, 2),
        "entry_inflation": round(len(cited_entries) / len(works), 2) if works else 0.0,
    }


def main() -> int:
    corpus = Path(sys.argv[1])
    rows = [density(Path(d), corpus) for d in sys.argv[2:]]
    hdr = f'{"run":26}{"words":>7}{"bib":>5}{"cited":>7}{"works":>7}{"works/1k":>10}{"inflation":>11}'
    print(hdr)
    print("-" * len(hdr))
    for r in rows:
        print(
            f'{r["run"]:26}{r["words"]:>7}{r["bib_entries"]:>5}{r["cited_entries"]:>7}'
            f'{r["distinct_works"]:>7}{r["works_per_1k_words"]:>10}{r["entry_inflation"]:>10}x'
        )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

content.text:
My instrument disagrees with Fable's hand-count (I get CTRL 14.96 / Rank7 11.39; Fable got 11.8 / 9.5), and one number gives the bug away: **`cited` equals `bib` for every arm** — every single entry appears "cited." That's not plausible; Fable counted 82 of 107 markers in Rank7's prose. The cause is that `report.md` ends with the numbered reference list itself, so my marker regex is reading the bibliography as if it were prose.

That's the same class of defect Fable caught in my last measurement — a metric that returns a number through a dead path. Fixing it to count markers in the **body only**.

    return []


def _work_key(entry: dict, work_of: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    ev = str(entry.get("evidence_id") or entry.get("ev_id") or "")
    if ev in work_of:
        return work_of[ev]
    doi = str(entry.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
    if doi:
        return f"doi:{doi}"
    title = _norm_title(entry.get("title") or "")
    if title:
        return f"t:{title}"
    return f"ev:{ev}"


def _same_work_map(corpus_path: Path) -> dict[str, str]:
    """ev_id -> work key, from the corpus's own same_work_groups.

    The groups arrive as DICTS with ``member_evidence_ids`` -- not bare id lists. A version
    of this that assumed bare lists would match nothing and silently report zero mirrors,
    which reads identical to a corpus that genuinely has none.
    """
    if not corpus_path.exists():
        return {}
    corpus = json.loads(corpus_path.read_text())
    groups = corpus.get("same_work_groups") if isinstance(corpus, dict) else None
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    for g in groups or []:
        if not isinstance(g, dict):
            continue
        members = g.get("member_evidence_ids") or []
        if not members:
            continue
        key = g.get("same_work_id") or g.get("canonical_index") or members[0]
        for m in members:
            out[str(m)] = f"w:{key}"
    return out


def density(run_dir: Path, corpus_path: Path) -> dict:
    report = (run_dir / "report.md").read_text()
    bib = json.loads((run_dir / "bibliography.json").read_text())
    entries = _entries(bib)
    work_of = _same_work_map(corpus_path)

    # BODY ONLY. report.md ends with a numbered "## References" list, so a marker regex over
    # the whole file re-reads the bibliography as prose and reports EVERY entry as cited --
    # cited==bib for every arm, which is the tell. Both the citation count and the word count
    # must exclude the reference list, or density is measured against the wrong denominator.
    body = _REFS_HEADING_RE.split(report)[0]

    # Prose cites by NUMERIC MARKER ([12]), never by ev_id -- an ev_\d+ regex over report.md
    # matches nothing and silently reports zero. Markers are 1-indexed into the bibliography.
    cited_markers = {int(m) for m in _MARKER_RE.findall(body)}
    cited_entries = [e for i, e in enumerate(entries, start=1) if i in cited_markers]

    words = len(body.split())
    works = {_work_key(e, work_of) for e in cited_entries}
    per_1k = 1000.0 * len(works) / words if words else 0.0
    return {
        "run": run_dir.name,
        "words": words,
        "bib_entries": len(entries),
        "cited_entries": len(cited_entries),
        "distinct_works": len(works),
        "works_per_1k_words": round(per_1k, 2),
        "entry_inflation": round(len(cited_entries) / len(works), 2) if works else 0.0,
    }


def main() -> int:
    corpus = Path(sys.argv[1])
    rows = [density(Path(d), corpus) for d in sys.argv[2:]]
    hdr = f'{"run":26}{"words":>7}{"bib":>5}{"cited":>7}{"works":>7}{"works/1k":>10}{"inflation":>11}'
    print(hdr)
    print("-" * len(hdr))
    for r in rows:
        print(
            f'{r["run"]:26}{r["words"]:>7}{r["bib_entries"]:>5}{r["cited_entries"]:>7}'
            f'{r["distinct_works"]:>7}{r["works_per_1k_words"]:>10}{r["entry_inflation"]:>10}x'
        )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

content.text:
Now the instrument is sound — and it **independently reproduces Fable's hand-count**: 82 cited markers in Rank7 (Fable counted 82), 77 distinct works (Fable estimated ~73). Two independent methods converging is the strongest check available here.

The corrected picture, on **body words only**:

| arm | body words | distinct works | **works / 1k words** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CTRL | 2,488 | 42 | **16.88** |
| ARMED | 3,418 | 51 | **14.92** |
| RANK8 (cap 15) | 3,610 | 41 | **11.36** |
| RANK7 (28-36) | 6,148 | 77 | **12.52** |

**Every depth lever lowers density.** Fable's direction is confirmed. But I'd push back on treating density as a maximand — by that logic the optimal report is 500 words citing 20 works. Density is a **guard against padding**, not a target: Rank7 cites *nearly double* the works (77 vs 42) across 2.5× the prose. The real test is whether density stays in a healthy band *while* length grows.

Let me commit the instrument and read Rank9.

The tell was cited==bib for all four arms. Both the citation count and the word

content.text:
**Rank9 is the best arm on every axis — and it broke the trade-off.** Stacking the menu cap on the depth lever raised length *and* density together:

| arm | words | verified | kept_f | distinct works | **works/1k** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CTRL | 3,408 | 81 | 0.519 | 42 | 16.88 |
| RANK7 (28-36) | 7,723 | 187 | 0.512 | 77 | 12.52 |
| **RANK9 (28-36 + cap30)** | **8,055** | **217** | **0.562** | **88** | **13.84** |

The cap recovered kept_fraction exactly as predicted (0.512 → 0.562), and density rose 12.52 → 13.84 *while the report got longer*. Cumulative: **3,408 → 8,055 words (2.36×), 42 → 88 distinct works (2.1×)**.

We're ~2k short of the 10k frontier floor. Fable's lever — more sections — is the direct path (10 sections × 21.7 verified sentences each; 14 sections at the same yield clears it). Let me find the section-count control.

def _theme_row_text(row: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
    """The text a THEME is derived from — the evidence row's OWN words only (title + statement +
    the direct_quote with URLs / non-letters stripped). NEVER the research question / task wording,
    so clustering is query-agnostic and cannot hardcode any single benchmark task's sections."""
    global _THEME_URL_RE  # noqa: PLW0603
    if _THEME_URL_RE is None:
        import re as _re  # noqa: PLC0415
        _THEME_URL_RE = _re.compile(
            r"https?://\S+|www\.\S+|\S+\.(?:com|org|net|edu|gov|io|co)\b"
        )
    import re as _re  # noqa: PLC0415
    dq = _THEME_URL_RE.sub(" ", str(row.get("direct_quote", "") or ""))
    dq = _re.sub(r"[^A-Za-z ]", " ", dq)
    return " ".join([
        str(row.get("title", "") or ""),
        str(row.get("statement", "") or ""),
        dq,
    ]).strip()


def _titlecase_terms(terms: list[str]) -> str:
    """Deterministic, human-ish section title from a cluster's top distinctive terms. Near-duplicates
    (regulation/regulatory, skill/skills) are collapsed by a shared 5-char prefix so a title never
    reads 'Regulation and Regulatory'; the first 2-3 distinct terms are joined readably."""

content.text:
**Found it — and it's the third purpose-built, default-off mechanism tonight.** `PG_OUTLINE_THEME_FLOOR` (outline_agent.py:2314, `default_on=False`) does *precisely* Fable's lever: it clusters the corpus query-agnostically and **adds a dedicated seed section for any large corpus theme the seed under-covers**, then pins the richer count so the loop can't merge it away. Themes are corpus-derived clusters, so each new section arrives with its **own disjoint evidence slice** — exactly the "disjoint per-section allocation" Fable specified. `PG_OUTLINE_THEME_FLOOR_MAX_NEW` defaults to 3.

Launching Rank10: the Rank9 config plus the theme floor, `MAX_NEW=4` → ~14 sections.

cat >> /home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md <<'EOF'

- 2026-07-13 00:25 | **RANK9 (STACKED) BROKE THE LENGTH-vs-DENSITY TRADE-OFF — BEST ARM ON EVERY AXIS.** clean + 28-36 + distinct15 + **cap=30**: **8055 words, 217 verified, kept_f 0.562, 115 bib, 88 distinct works, density 13.84/1k.** The cap did exactly what Rank8 predicted it would — **recovered kept_fraction 0.512 -> 0.562** — and, the part I did NOT expect, **density ROSE 12.52 -> 13.84 WHILE the report got LONGER.** Cumulative vs baseline: **3408 -> 8055 words (2.36x), 42 -> 88 distinct works (2.1x)**, density 16.88 -> 13.84 (-18%). The two levers are **independent and they stack**: the sentence target buys length, the menu cap buys the *quality of what gets written*.
- 2026-07-13 00:25 | **DENSITY INSTRUMENT BUILT — AND IT CAUGHT ITS OWN BUG BEFORE I SHIPPED A NUMBER** (`scripts/flywheel_density.py`). Measures Fable's pre-registered falsifier: **distinct WORKS (not bib entries) per 1000 BODY words**, collapsing mirrors via the corpus's own `same_work_groups` -> DOI -> normalized title. **The bug: `report.md` ENDS WITH THE NUMBERED `## References` LIST**, so a marker regex over the whole file re-reads the bibliography as prose and reports **every entry as cited** — the tell was `cited == bib` for all four arms. Fixed to body-only (both numerator and denominator). **Corrected, it independently reproduces Fable's hand-count: 82 cited markers in rank7 (Fable counted 82), 77 distinct works (Fable estimated ~73).** Two independent methods converging is the strongest check available here. *This is the second measurement of mine tonight that returned a plausible number through a dead code path — the pattern is now the thing to watch, not the individual bug.*
- 2026-07-13 00:25 | HONEST NOTE ON DENSITY AS A TARGET: density FALLS with every depth lever (CTRL 16.88 is the *densest* arm). I am **not** treating it as a maximand — by that logic the optimal report is 500 words citing 20 works, and CTRL "wins" by being short. It is a **PADDING GUARD**: the question is whether density stays in a healthy band *while* length grows. Rank9 answers yes (density UP vs Rank7 at greater length).
- 2026-07-13 00:30 | **MY MIRROR-DOMINATION HYPOTHESIS: REFUTED BY MY OWN MEASUREMENT (and it also refutes part of Fable's diagnosis).** I predicted the writer milks 8 sources because its menu is mirror-dominated. Measured the rank7 outline against the corpus's 55 same_work_groups: **595 section rows -> 527 distinct works, only ~11% mirrors.** The menus are NOT mirror-dominated. And the sections HAVE the breadth: Introduction **166 distinct works available -> 7 cited**; Productivity **57 -> 8**; Empirical **40 -> 5**; Theoretical **15 -> 8 (with 10 sentences on [1] alone)**. **7 of 10 sections have >=15 distinct works available, so rule #9 is SATISFIABLE there** — which **refutes Fable's "the binding constraint is distinct usable evidence per section"** for those 7. The writer is not starved; **it under-uses breadth it already has**, and an oversized menu is what drives the milking (the same overload effect Rank8 proved). That is why the *cap* — not more evidence — was the thing that raised density.
- 2026-07-13 00:35 | **RANK10 LAUNCHED — AND THE LEVER WAS ALREADY IN THE CODE, DEFAULT-OFF: `PG_OUTLINE_THEME_FLOOR` (outline_agent.py:2314, `default_on=False`).** It does *precisely* Fable's prescription: clusters the corpus **query-agnostically** and ADDS a dedicated seed section for any large corpus theme the seed under-covers, then the RACE-FLOOR pins the richer count so the agent loop cannot merge it away. Corpus-derived clusters => each new section arrives with its **own disjoint evidence slice** = "disjoint per-section allocation", already built. **This is the THIRD purpose-built mechanism found dark behind a default-off flag tonight** (after `PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION`, which alone reproduced a 42-minute judge, and `PG_CROSS_SECTION_REPETITION_GUARD`, still off). **The pattern is now the headline finding: this codebase's biggest wins are not missing — they are BUILT, TESTED, AND SWITCHED OFF.** Rank10 = Rank9 + THEME_FLOOR=1 + MAX_NEW=4 -> ~14 sections. At Rank9's 21.7 verified sentences/section that is ~300 verified => **~11k words, i.e. the frontier floor.**
EOF

content.text:
**The theme floor fired and added zero sections** — and its reasoning is worth more than the sections would have been. It clustered the corpus and found every salient theme already covered; the *uncovered* clusters it skipped as `low_salience_artifact` are:

- 169 rows: `writing / students / marketing / academic / books`
- 54 rows: `legal / paralegal / paralegals / law / lawyer`

Those are exactly the alien rows — the paralegal reading lists. **So "more sections" is exhausted on this corpus: it honestly has ~9–10 salient themes, and the only way to reach 14 would be to make sections out of junk** — which is the padding we're guarding against. Fable's lever is right in principle and unavailable in fact.

It also exposed a real bug: the cluster sizes sum to 772 of 997 rows, so the **theme floor is clustering the *pre-judge* evidence**, not the cleaned pool.

Since the theme floor was a no-op, Rank10 is now effectively a **replication of Rank9** — which hands me the run-to-run variance estimate I've been missing all night (every arm so far is n=1).

Those are **exactly the alien rows** (the paralegal reading lists). **CONCLUSION: "more sections" is EXHAUSTED on this corpus.** It honestly decomposes into ~9-10 salient themes; reaching Fable's 13-14 would mean **building sections out of junk** — the precise padding the whole density guard exists to prevent. **Fable's lever is right in principle and UNAVAILABLE IN FACT.** The mechanism refused to pad, which is the behaviour I want from it.
- 2026-07-13 00:45 | BUG FOUND IN PASSING (theme floor x judge interaction): the floor's clusters sum to **772 of 997 rows** and include the paralegal/student-writing themes the judge had *already deleted* — so **`_derive_theme_coverage_sections` clusters the PRE-JUDGE evidence**, not the cleaned pool. Harmless here only because the low-salience skip caught the junk anyway; on a different corpus it would happily seed a section from deleted rows. Logged, not yet fixed.
- 2026-07-13 00:45 | **UNPLANNED GIFT: Rank10 is now a REPLICATION of Rank9** (theme floor = no-op => identical effective config). Every arm tonight has been **n=1**, and I have twice had to reason about noise (the +0.085 kept_fraction, the word-count spread) **without any variance estimate at all**. This run supplies one for free. **I will read Rank10 as a variance probe, not as a new arm** — and any Rank9-vs-Rank10 gap is the noise floor against which every claim tonight must be re-read.
EOF

were 169 rows of writing/students/marketing/academic/books and 54 rows of

So Fable's 'more sections (13-14)' lever is right in principle and UNAVAILABLE in
fact: this corpus honestly decomposes into ~9-10 salient themes, and reaching 14
would mean building sections out of paralegal reading lists -- the exact padding
the density guard exists to prevent. The mechanism refused to pad. Good.

Bug found in passing: the floor's clusters sum to 772 of 997 rows and include
themes the judge had already deleted, so _derive_theme_coverage_sections clusters
the PRE-JUDGE evidence. Harmless here (the low-salience skip caught the junk), but

content.text:
**The variance probe changes how I must read tonight's results — and it forces me to withdraw a claim.** Same config, two runs:

| metric | Rank9 | Rank10 | spread |
|---|---|---|---|
| verified sentences | 217 | 219 | **±1%** (stable) |
| kept_fraction | 0.562 | 0.572 | **±0.01** (stable) |
| report words | 8,055 | 9,300 | **±15%** |
| body words | 6,359 | 7,742 | **±22%** |
| density | 13.84 | 12.14 | **±12%** |

**Verified-sentence count and kept_fraction are stable; word count and density are not.** That means my claim that "the cap raised density 12.52 → 13.84" is **inside the noise floor (12%) and must be withdrawn** — I can't distinguish it from run-to-run variance.

But it cuts the other way too, and I have to follow it honestly: with kept_fraction's noise at ~±0.01, the CTRL→ARMED move of **+0.085 is ~8× the noise** — so **Fable's prediction (|Δ| ≤ 0.05) is refuted after all**, and my "unresolved, ~1.5σ" verdict was over-cautious. My binomial model assumed sentence-level independence and *overestimated* the true variance.

The effects that survive the noise floor are the big ones: **3,408 → 9,300 words (2.7×), 81 → 219 verified sentences (2.7×, on the *stable* metric), 42 → 94 distinct works (2.2×)**.

input.command:
cat >> /home/polaris/polaris_project/FLYWHEEL_PROGRESS.md <<'EOF'

- 2026-07-13 01:35 | **THE VARIANCE PROBE (Rank10 = Rank9 replicate) — AND IT FORCES ME TO WITHDRAW A CLAIM AND REINSTATE ONE I HAD KILLED.** Same effective config, two runs: verified **217 vs 219 (±1%)**, kept_fraction **0.562 vs 0.572 (±0.01)** — but report words **8055 vs 9300 (±15%)**, body words **6359 vs 7742 (±22%)**, density **13.84 vs 12.14 (±12%)**. **VERIFIED-SENTENCE COUNT AND KEPT_FRACTION ARE STABLE; WORD COUNT AND DENSITY ARE NOT.** Consequences, both directions:
  - **WITHDRAWN: "the cap raised density 12.52 -> 13.84."** That is **+1.32 against a measured noise floor of 1.70** — inside the noise. I cannot distinguish it from run-to-run variance and I am not going to pretend otherwise. (The stacked cap's kept_fraction gain, 0.512 -> 0.562/0.572 against ±0.01 noise, **does** survive — the cap is real, my *reason* for liking it was not.)
  - **REINSTATED — AND IT WAS *MY* CAUTION THAT WAS WRONG:** I logged the CTRL->ARMED kept_fraction move (+0.085) as "~1.5σ, unresolved noise" using a **binomial SE of 0.055**. The empirical replicate says the true noise on kept_fraction is **~±0.01**, so +0.085 is **~8x the noise: Fable's prediction (|Δ|<=0.05) IS REFUTED.** My binomial model assumed sentence-level independence and **overestimated** the variance (Fable argued clustering would make it *bigger*; the measurement says smaller). **Both of us reasoned about noise for hours without ever measuring it. One replicate settled it in 50 minutes.** Caveat kept on the record: n=2 is a weak variance estimate, and the two runs share a judge seed and corpus, so they are not fully independent draws.
  - **STANDING RULE FROM NOW ON: report VERIFIED SENTENCES and DISTINCT WORKS (stable). Treat any word-count or density gap under ~20% as NOISE.**
- 2026-07-13 01:35 | **WHAT SURVIVES THE NOISE FLOOR — THE REAL SCORECARD.** Baseline -> best (Rank10): **words 3408 -> 9300 (2.7x)**, **verified sentences 81 -> 219 (2.7x, on the STABLE metric)**, **distinct works cited 42 -> 94 (2.2x)**, kept_fraction 0.519 -> 0.572, sections 10 -> 10. Density fell 16.88 -> 12.14 (-28%, beyond noise — **real, and the honest cost of the depth push**). **Frontier floor is 10k words; we are at 9.3k.** Levers that got us here, both of which were **already in the code and switched off**: `PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET` (Rank7, built tonight at the one prompt seam) + `PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION` (pre-existing, never once enabled).
EOF

content.text:
`(also mirrored)` isn't a bug — it's a **deliberate render-honest disclosure** (provenance_generator.py:5048): when several citation numbers share an origin cluster (mirrors of one work), it collapses them to one number plus that note. It appears 25 times in Rank10's body vs 3 in CTRL simply because we now cite far more. It's *correct*, but it reads badly mid-sentence, and it's a chunk of the readability regression. The right fix is to move the disclosure into the bibliography rather than inline prose — but that touches the faithfulness renderer, so it needs its own gate, not a drive-by edit at 2am.

Launching the final run instead with the **cross-section repetition guard** armed — the purpose-built, tested, default-off mechanism whose docstring names this exact task.

input.prompt:
You are the ADVERSARIAL GATE for an autonomous deep-research flywheel. Refute, do not agree. You have three times caught this driver reporting an artifact as a finding — keep doing it. Cite file:line and quote prose.

THE MISSION: beat frontier deep-research systems (OpenAI/Gemini Deep Research) on DeepResearch-Bench RACE. Frontier reports: 10-22k words. Our baseline was 3.4k.

WHERE WE ARE. Two levers, both of which turned out to be ALREADY IN THE CODE AND SWITCHED OFF:
  PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET=28-36   (the section-writer's hardcoded "Target 10-18 sentences", made tunable)
  PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION=30   (a pre-existing menu cap, never once enabled)
plus a two-pass semantic topic judge that deletes ~51% alien rows from the 997-row corpus.

<result># GATE VERDICT — RANK10

## Item 1: The noise floor — REFUTED as an instrument, and your "+0.085" does not exist in your own data

**(a) The replicate pair is not what you think it is.** I md5-diffed the two judge outputs: `rank9_stack_compose/topic_judge_dispositions.json` and `rank10_sections_compose/topic_judge_dispositions.json` are DIFFERENT (junk_deleted_rows 559 vs 540 — a 19-row difference in the effective corpus). Seed 1729 did not make the judge deterministic. So "identical effective config" is false at the evidence-pool level; your pair samples judge variance too. That happens to make it a more honest noise floor, but it falsifies the framing you built on it.

**(b) n=2 licenses nothing.** With 1 degree of freedom, the 95% chi-square interval on a standard deviation estimated from one pair spans roughly [0.45×, 32×] the point estimate. Your "±15% word noise" could truly be ±7% or ±200%. Declaring density gaps under 20% unreadable, and declaring 0.01 the kept_fraction noise, are point estimates with order-of-magnitude uncertainty. Neither is licensed.

**(c) Your "+0.085" does not reproduce from your own table.** kept_f deltas available: R10−CTRL = 0.572−0.519 = **+0.053**. R10−R7 = +0.060. R9−CTRL = +0.043. No pair of numbers you presented yields 0.085. The largest observed delta (+0.060) is right at the boundary of my |Δ|≤0.05 prediction, not "8x the noise" beyond it. Show me which two numbers you subtracted. Until then the reinstated refutation is REJECTED — built on an irreproducible number and an n=2 variance estimate.

**(d) kept_fraction IS partially pinned.** `multi_section_generator.py:9723` has `min_kept_fraction: float = 0.5` (note: **0.5, not the 0.40 you stated** — check which one actually ran), and lines 6525-6560 implement a best-of-two regen for any section below it. This censors the left tail per section, mechanically compressing variance and pulling arm aggregates toward ≥0.5. CTRL sits at 0.519 — just above the floor, consistent with active rescue. Cross-arm kept_fraction deltas partially measure retry-rescue frequency, not free writer quality. Your suspicion is correct.

## Item 2: RACE scores — the 5,900 words moved essentially nothing

| Dimension | CTRL | RANK7 (last gate) | RANK10 | Frontier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Comprehensiveness | 3 | 4 | **4** | 4-5 |
| Insight/depth | 3 | 3 | **3** | 4-5 |
| Instruction-following | 2 | 3 | **3** | 4 |
| Readability | 3 | 2 | **2** | 4-5 |

- **Comprehensiveness 4 (unchanged from RANK7):** real breadth added — 4IR framing, ILO digital-divide numbers, gender, policy instruments. But it's bought with the corpus's junk tail: of the 42 bibliography entries added over CTRL, only **5 are T1/T2** and 27 are T4+/UNKNOWN.
- **Insight 3 (unchanged):** the section titled "Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions" (report.md:33-35) contains **zero synthesis and zero contradictions** — it is another evidence dump, opening with a truncated non-sentence. Meanwhile an actual unflagged contradiction sits in the text: robot density in 2023 is "141 robots per 10,000 employees" at line 15 ([33]) and "162 units per 10,000 employees" at line 43 ([101]), and the Limitations section states "No contradictions were detected by the pipeline." A frontier report reconciles that in a table; yours doesn't notice it.
- **Instruction-following 3:** "Fourth Industrial Revolution" now appears **5+ times in the body** (lines 7, 23) vs zero in CTRL — that fix landed, credit given. But task 72's third explicit instruction is *"Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles"* and RANK10 cites a Toptal freelancer-marketing listicle [55], an amplifai blog [99], apporya.com [91], a Substack [52], hrexecutive.com [54], and a personal-site mirror [85]; 59 of 105 references are T4/T5/T6/T7/UNKNOWN (CTRL: 32 of 63). The longer report violates the hard constraint MORE, in absolute count and in mix.
- **Readability 2:** "(also mirrored)" appears **25 times inline in the prose** (CTRL: 3) — `provenance_generator.py:5044-5048` appends it by design, but a RACE grader reads it as pipeline garbage. Three broken section openers: the report's first body sentence is "Within this framework..." with no framework yet introduced (line 7); section 4 opens "The study also documents..." with no antecedent (line 19); section 8 opens with the truncated fragment "Bureau of Labor Statistics data or projections for the idea of a general acceleration of job loss..." (line 35). Plus "Census Bureau survey of 300,000 businesses finding that..." (verbless, line 23), a missing terminal period at line 39, and ten 700-1200-word single-paragraph walls with zero tables, zero subheads. This is where you lose to frontier hardest.

## Item 3: Padding — ~28% of added words, same disease as RANK7, plus a scaled-up annotation leak; the guard will catch ~none of it

Added body words: 7742 − 2488 = 5,254. Buckets:
- **Cross-section paraphrase duplicates ≈ 550-600w:** GPTs-are-GPTs 1.8%/46% appears near-verbatim at line 15 ([27]) and line 23 ([50]); Noy-Zhang "2x / 1.6x" at line 19 ([41]) and again at line 27 ([61]) as "2 times / 1.6 times"; Goldman's 2.5%-at-risk at line 23 ([52]) and line 43 ([96]); the 5.4% firm-adoption figure **three times** (lines 35, 43×2 — including two adjacent sentences citing [94] then [95] as if they corroborate, when both are the same Census BTOS finding); [82]'s "inconclusive... claims premature" verbatim-modulo-lead-in at lines 35 and 39; [84]'s matching-effects sentence at lines 35 and 39.
- **Off-topic strays ≈ 600w:** Toptal content-writer marketing, AI-lit-reviews-getting-rejected, academic-writing systematic review [92], tourism/hospitality [10], three sentences of ALMATO RPA case minutiae [36], supply-chain paper-counting [103], Industry 4.0 "Reasons For/Against" [83].
- **Metadata-as-content ≈ 200w:** "published in the Strategic Management Journal" (line 15), "published in the Journal of Economic Perspectives" (line 11), "major contributions from China, the US, and Germany" [31], "found 525 papers... only 20 addressed" [103], and section 2's closing sentence — "The cited work presents a task-based framework for analyzing technology's implications..." — which is a citation describing itself.
- **New defect:** one **misattribution**: line 43, "That same study observed a 3.7-minute decrease in average handle time" refers grammatically to the Danish chatbot study but cites [104] — Brynjolfsson's *Generative AI at Work*, whose same stat (3.8 min, 9%) already appears at line 35 as [71]. Same work, same finding, two versions, once misattributed.

**Guard prediction: it will catch approximately zero of these.** Its own docstring is explicit (`cross_section_repetition_guard.py:20-24): "Two sentences that differ in ANY content — a year, a figure, an entity, directionality, an extra clause — have DIFFERENT normalized text and are NEVER clustered."* Every duplicate above is a paraphrase ("2x" vs "2 times", split vs fused sentences). I found zero byte-identical cross-section restatements in RANK10. Expected consolidations from your in-flight run: **0-2, under 100 words**. Do not wait on it.

## Item 4: Completion criterion — and stop saying 9.3k

Your "9300 words" includes ~1,550 words of reference lines. **Body is 7,742** — that's the number comparable to frontier's 10-22k. You are not at the frontier floor; you're 2.3k under it, measured honestly.

Stable criterion (all replicate-stable or deterministic):
1. **Verified sentences ≥ 300** (your one genuinely stable metric; currently 219) — this drives body ≥ ~9-10k mechanically.
2. **TRUE distinct works ≥ 100, corpus-keyed density ≥ 13/1k** (see Item 5 — the current instrument is broken; headroom provably exists).
3. **Artifact-zero:** 0 "(also mirrored)" in prose, 0 truncated/verbless sentences, 0 antecedent-less section openers (currently 25 / ≥4 / 3).
4. **Tier compliance ≥ 80% T1-T3 among cited entries** (currently ~44/97) — this is an explicit task instruction, and RACE instruction-following grades it.
5. Ultimate criterion: run the RACE rubric judge head-to-head vs a frontier report on task 72. Words are a proxy; you've maxed the proxy's noise, so stop steering on it.

## Item 5: The thing you are most wrong about — and I measured it

You are most wrong about **your density number, and therefore about where the ceiling is**. I reran your instrument and then re-keyed cited entries through their own corpus rows (evidence_id → corpus DOI/title — strictly better keying than `flywheel_density.py`'s entry-level fallback, which matches same_work_groups for only **16 of 97** cited entries and is defeated by "[PDF]" prefixes and 70-char "..." truncations):

- CTRL: script 42 works @ 16.88/1k → **true 41 @ 16.48** (barely moved)
- RANK10: script 94 works @ 12.14/1k → **true 68 @ 8.78** (overcounted 38%)

Your instrument's failure is asymmetric and flatters exactly the arm you're selling: [1],[13],[14],[16] are all Acemoglu-Restrepo 2019; [42],[78],[85],[86],[104] are all Brynjolfsson — counted as ~9 works. The true CTRL→RANK10 density collapse is **47%, not 28%**. This is the fourth artifact-reported-as-finding.

And it inverts your hypothesis (a): the corpus is **NOT** the binding constraint. Measured: 509 rows survive the judge, containing **147 distinct works with a T1-T3 row; RANK10 cites 68 works total and leaves 112 quality works completely uncited** — while citing a Toptal listicle. The binding constraint is **evidence selection into the writer menu**, which is feeding the low-tier tail into your cap30 slots.

**The ONE next action:** fix `_work_key` to key through the corpus row (5 lines), then rerun the RANK9 config once with the writer menu ranked tier-first. **Falsifying number: if that run's corpus-keyed density is still ≤ 10/1k at ≥ 7k body words, selection wasn't the constraint and the problem is the writer's copying behavior itself.** If it clears 13/1k, you just found the real lever — and it was never a sentence target.</result>

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Insight-density instrument: distinct WORKS cited per 1000 words.

Fable's pre-registered falsifier for the depth levers. Word count alone cannot tell a
deeper report from a padded one -- a run can double its length by restating the same
finding against more sources. Density can: it only rises if the report brings in
genuinely new works.

WORKS, NOT BIBLIOGRAPHY ENTRIES. The bibliography over-counts: one paper occupies several
entries when the corpus holds mirror rows of it (Brynjolfsson/Li/Raymond took SIX slots in
rank7). Reporting entries as "distinct sources" is how a 73-work report gets sold as a
107-source one. Collapse mirrors first, three ways, in order:
  1. same_work_groups from the corpus (authoritative -- the corpus already knows).
  2. DOI, when present (authoritative across mirrors).
  3. normalized title (the fallback: lowercased, stripped of punctuation and an "(also
     mirrored)" suffix, truncated -- catches mirrors the corpus never grouped).
Only entries actually CITED in the prose count. An uncited bibliography entry is not a
source the report used; counting it would re-inflate exactly the number this exists to
deflate.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"\[(\d+)\]")
_PUNCT_RE = re.compile(r"[^\w\s]")
_WS_RE = re.compile(r"\s+")
_MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE = re.compile(r"\(also mirrored\)", re.IGNORECASE)
# The reference list is part of report.md. Everything from this heading on is NOT prose.
_REFS_HEADING_RE = re.compile(r"^#{1,3}\s+(references|bibliography)\s*$", re.IGNORECASE | re.MULTILINE)


def _norm_title(title: str) -> str:
    t = _MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE.sub(" ", str(title or "")).lower()
    t = _PUNCT_RE.sub(" ", t)
    t = _WS_RE.sub(" ", t).strip()
    return t[:70]


def _entries(bib: object) -> list[dict]:
    if isinstance(bib, list):
        return [e for e in bib if isinstance(e, dict)]
    if isinstance(bib, dict):
        for key in ("entries", "bibliography", "items"):
            v = bib.get(key)
            if isinstance(v, list):
                return [e for e in v if isinstance(e, dict)]
    return []


def _work_key(entry: dict, work_of: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    ev = str(entry.get("evidence_id") or entry.get("ev_id") or "")
    if ev in work_of:
        return work_of[ev]
    doi = str(entry.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
    if doi:
        return f"doi:{doi}"
    title = _norm_title(entry.get("title") or "")
    if title:
        return f"t:{title}"
    return f"ev:{ev}"


def _same_work_map(corpus_path: Path) -> dict[str, str]:
    """ev_id -> work key, from the corpus's own same_work_groups.

    The groups arrive as DICTS with ``member_evidence_ids`` -- not bare id lists. A version
    of this that assumed bare lists would match nothing and silently report zero mirrors,
    which reads identical to a corpus that genuinely has none.
    """
    if not corpus_path.exists():
        return {}
    corpus = json.loads(corpus_path.read_text())
    groups = corpus.get("same_work_groups") if isinstance(corpus, dict) else None
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    for g in groups or []:
        if not isinstance(g, dict):
            continue
        members = g.get("member_evidence_ids") or []
        if not members:
            continue
        key = g.get("same_work_id") or g.get("canonical_index") or members[0]
        for m in members:
            out[str(m)] = f"w:{key}"
    return out


def density(run_dir: Path, corpus_path: Path) -> dict:
    report = (run_dir / "report.md").read_text()
    bib = json.loads((run_dir / "bibliography.json").read_text())
    entries = _entries(bib)
    work_of = _same_work_map(corpus_path)

    # BODY ONLY. report.md ends with a numbered "## References" list, so a marker regex over
    # the whole file re-reads the bibliography as prose and reports EVERY entry as cited --
    # cited==bib for every arm, which is the tell. Both the citation count and the word count
    # must exclude the reference list, or density is measured against the wrong denominator.
    body = _REFS_HEADING_RE.split(report)[0]

    # Prose cites by NUMERIC MARKER ([12]), never by ev_id -- an ev_\d+ regex over report.md
    # matches nothing and silently reports zero. Markers are 1-indexed into the bibliography.
    cited_markers = {int(m) for m in _MARKER_RE.findall(body)}
    cited_entries = [e for i, e in enumerate(entries, start=1) if i in cited_markers]

    words = len(body.split())

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Insight-density instrument: distinct WORKS cited per 1000 words.

Fable's pre-registered falsifier for the depth levers. Word count alone cannot tell a
deeper report from a padded one -- a run can double its length by restating the same
finding against more sources. Density can: it only rises if the report brings in
genuinely new works.

WORKS, NOT BIBLIOGRAPHY ENTRIES. The bibliography over-counts: one paper occupies several
entries when the corpus holds mirror rows of it (Brynjolfsson/Li/Raymond took SIX slots in
rank7). Reporting entries as "distinct sources" is how a 73-work report gets sold as a
107-source one. Collapse mirrors first, three ways, in order:
  1. same_work_groups from the corpus (authoritative -- the corpus already knows).
  2. DOI, when present (authoritative across mirrors).
  3. normalized title (the fallback: lowercased, stripped of punctuation and an "(also
     mirrored)" suffix, truncated -- catches mirrors the corpus never grouped).
Only entries actually CITED in the prose count. An uncited bibliography entry is not a
source the report used; counting it would re-inflate exactly the number this exists to
deflate.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"\[(\d+)\]")
_PUNCT_RE = re.compile(r"[^\w\s]")
_WS_RE = re.compile(r"\s+")
_MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE = re.compile(r"\(also mirrored\)", re.IGNORECASE)
# The reference list is part of report.md. Everything from this heading on is NOT prose.
_REFS_HEADING_RE = re.compile(r"^#{1,3}\s+(references|bibliography)\s*$", re.IGNORECASE | re.MULTILINE)


def _norm_title(title: str) -> str:
    t = _MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE.sub(" ", str(title or "")).lower()
    t = _PUNCT_RE.sub(" ", t)
    t = _WS_RE.sub(" ", t).strip()
    return t[:70]


def _entries(bib: object) -> list[dict]:
    if isinstance(bib, list):
        return [e for e in bib if isinstance(e, dict)]
    if isinstance(bib, dict):
        for key in ("entries", "bibliography", "items"):
            v = bib.get(key)
            if isinstance(v, list):
                return [e for e in v if isinstance(e, dict)]
    return []


def _work_key(entry: dict, work_of: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    """Collapse an entry to the WORK it represents.

    The title lives under ``source_title``. The first cut of this read ``title`` -- a key that
    does not exist on these entries -- so every title normalized to "" and the fallback keyed
    each entry by its own ev_id: NO mirror was ever collapsed. It reported 94 works where there
    are 68, and it inflated the long arms most (they cite more mirrors), i.e. it flattered
    exactly the arm being sold. Order matters: same_work_groups covers only ~16 of ~97 cited
    entries, and DOI is absent on the mirrors -- the T4 "[PDF] ..." scrape of a T1 journal paper
    carries neither. Normalized title is what actually does the work here, so it must be tried.
    """
    ev = str(entry.get("evidence_id") or entry.get("ev_id") or "")
    if ev in work_of:
        return work_of[ev]
    doi = str(entry.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
    if doi:
        return f"doi:{doi}"
    title = _norm_title(entry.get("source_title") or entry.get("title") or "")
    if title:
        return f"t:{title}"
    return f"ev:{ev}"


def _same_work_map(corpus_path: Path) -> dict[str, str]:
    """ev_id -> work key, from the corpus's own same_work_groups.

    The groups arrive as DICTS with ``member_evidence_ids`` -- not bare id lists. A version
    of this that assumed bare lists would match nothing and silently report zero mirrors,
    which reads identical to a corpus that genuinely has none.
    """
    if not corpus_path.exists():
        return {}
    corpus = json.loads(corpus_path.read_text())
    groups = corpus.get("same_work_groups") if isinstance(corpus, dict) else None
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    for g in groups or []:
        if not isinstance(g, dict):
            continue
        members = g.get("member_evidence_ids") or []
        if not members:
            continue
        key = g.get("same_work_id") or g.get("canonical_index") or members[0]
        for m in members:
            out[str(m)] = f"w:{key}"
    return out


def density(run_dir: Path, corpus_path: Path) -> dict:
    report = (run_dir / "report.md").read_text()
    bib = json.loads((run_dir / "bibliography.json").read_text())
    entries = _entries(bib)
    work_of = _same_work_map(corpus_path)

    # BODY ONLY. report.md ends with a numbered "## References" list, so a marker regex over
    # the whole file re-reads the bibliography as prose and reports EVERY entry as cited --
    # cited==bib for every arm, which is the tell. Both the citation count and the word count
    # must exclude the reference list, or density is measured against the wrong denominator.
    body = _REFS_HEADING_RE.split(report)[0]

    # Prose cites by NUMERIC MARKER ([12]), never by ev_id -- an ev_\d+ regex over report.md
    # matches nothing and silently reports zero. Markers are 1-indexed into the bibliography.
    cited_markers = {int(m) for m in _MARKER_RE.findall(body)}
    cited_entries = [e for i, e in enumerate(entries, start=1) if i in cited_markers]

    words = len(body.split())
    works = {_work_key(e, work_of) for e in cited_entries}
    per_1k = 1000.0 * len(works) / words if words else 0.0
    return {
        "run": run_dir.name,
        "words": words,
        "bib_entries": len(entries),
        "cited_entries": len(cited_entries),
        "distinct_works": len(works),
        "works_per_1k_words": round(per_1k, 2),
        "entry_inflation": round(len(cited_entries) / len(works), 2) if works else 0.0,
    }


def main() -> int:
    corpus = Path(sys.argv[1])
    rows = [density(Path(d), corpus) for d in sys.argv[2:]]
    hdr = f'{"run":26}{"words":>7}{"bib":>5}{"cited":>7}{"works":>7}{"works/1k":>10}{"inflation":>11}'
    print(hdr)
    print("-" * len(hdr))
    for r in rows:
        print(
            f'{r["run"]:26}{r["words"]:>7}{r["bib_entries"]:>5}{r["cited_entries"]:>7}'
            f'{r["distinct_works"]:>7}{r["works_per_1k_words"]:>10}{r["entry_inflation"]:>10}x'
        )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

# Scrape prefixes a search engine prepends to a mirrored copy of a paper ("[PDF] Automation ...").
_SCRAPE_PREFIX_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*\[(pdf|html|doc|epub)\]\s*", re.IGNORECASE)
# DRB task 72 instructs: cite only high-quality, English-language JOURNAL articles. Tier compliance
# is therefore an instruction-following metric, not a nicety -- RACE grades it.
_QUALITY_TIERS = frozenset({"T1", "T2", "T3"})


def _norm_title(title: str) -> str:
    """Normalize a title so a work's MIRRORS collapse onto it.

    ``[PDF]``/``[HTML]`` scrape prefixes must go BEFORE punctuation stripping: otherwise
    "[PDF] Automation and New Tasks" keeps a leading "pdf" token and never matches the T1
    journal entry it is a mirror of -- which is precisely how a T4 preprint scrape got counted
    as a work distinct from the paper it copies.
    """
    t = _MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE.sub(" ", str(title or ""))
    t = _SCRAPE_PREFIX_RE.sub(" ", t).lower()
    t = _PUNCT_RE.sub(" ", t)
    t = _WS_RE.sub(" ", t).strip()
    return t[:70]


def _entries(bib: object) -> list[dict]:
    if isinstance(bib, list):
        return [e for e in bib if isinstance(e, dict)]
    if isinstance(bib, dict):
        for key in ("entries", "bibliography", "items"):
            v = bib.get(key)
            if isinstance(v, list):
                return [e for e in v if isinstance(e, dict)]
    return []


def _work_key(entry: dict, work_of: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    """Collapse an entry to the WORK it represents.

    The title lives under ``source_title``. The first cut of this read ``title`` -- a key that
    does not exist on these entries -- so every title normalized to "" and the fallback keyed
    each entry by its own ev_id: NO mirror was ever collapsed. It reported 94 works where there
    are 68, and it inflated the long arms most (they cite more mirrors), i.e. it flattered
    exactly the arm being sold. Order matters: same_work_groups covers only ~16 of ~97 cited
    entries, and DOI is absent on the mirrors -- the T4 "[PDF] ..." scrape of a T1 journal paper
    carries neither. Normalized title is what actually does the work here, so it must be tried.
    """
    ev = str(entry.get("evidence_id") or entry.get("ev_id") or "")
    if ev in work_of:
        return work_of[ev]
    doi = str(entry.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
    if doi:
        return f"doi:{doi}"
    title = _norm_title(entry.get("source_title") or entry.get("title") or "")
    if title:
        return f"t:{title}"
    return f"ev:{ev}"


def _same_work_map(corpus_path: Path) -> dict[str, str]:
    """ev_id -> work key, from the corpus's own same_work_groups.

    The groups arrive as DICTS with ``member_evidence_ids`` -- not bare id lists. A version
    of this that assumed bare lists would match nothing and silently report zero mirrors,
    which reads identical to a corpus that genuinely has none.
    """
    if not corpus_path.exists():
        return {}
    corpus = json.loads(corpus_path.read_text())
    groups = corpus.get("same_work_groups") if isinstance(corpus, dict) else None
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    for g in groups or []:
        if not isinstance(g, dict):
            continue
        members = g.get("member_evidence_ids") or []
        if not members:
            continue
        key = g.get("same_work_id") or g.get("canonical_index") or members[0]
        for m in members:
            out[str(m)] = f"w:{key}"
    return out


def density(run_dir: Path, corpus_path: Path) -> dict:
    report = (run_dir / "report.md").read_text()
    bib = json.loads((run_dir / "bibliography.json").read_text())
    entries = _entries(bib)
    work_of = _same_work_map(corpus_path)

    # BODY ONLY. report.md ends with a numbered "## References" list, so a marker regex over
    # the whole file re-reads the bibliography as prose and reports EVERY entry as cited --
    # cited==bib for every arm, which is the tell. Both the citation count and the word count
    # must exclude the reference list, or density is measured against the wrong denominator.
    body = _REFS_HEADING_RE.split(report)[0]

    # Prose cites by NUMERIC MARKER ([12]), never by ev_id -- an ev_\d+ regex over report.md
    # matches nothing and silently reports zero. Markers are 1-indexed into the bibliography.
    cited_markers = {int(m) for m in _MARKER_RE.findall(body)}
    cited_entries = [e for i, e in enumerate(entries, start=1) if i in cited_markers]

    words = len(body.split())
    works = {_work_key(e, work_of) for e in cited_entries}
    per_1k = 1000.0 * len(works) / words if words else 0.0
    return {
        "run": run_dir.name,
        "words": words,
        "bib_entries": len(entries),
        "cited_entries": len(cited_entries),
        "distinct_works": len(works),
        "works_per_1k_words": round(per_1k, 2),
        "entry_inflation": round(len(cited_entries) / len(works), 2) if works else 0.0,
    }


def main() -> int:
    corpus = Path(sys.argv[1])
    rows = [density(Path(d), corpus) for d in sys.argv[2:]]
    hdr = f'{"run":26}{"words":>7}{"bib":>5}{"cited":>7}{"works":>7}{"works/1k":>10}{"inflation":>11}'
    print(hdr)
    print("-" * len(hdr))
    for r in rows:
        print(
            f'{r["run"]:26}{r["words"]:>7}{r["bib_entries"]:>5}{r["cited_entries"]:>7}'
            f'{r["distinct_works"]:>7}{r["works_per_1k_words"]:>10}{r["entry_inflation"]:>10}x'
        )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Insight-density instrument: distinct WORKS cited per 1000 words.

Fable's pre-registered falsifier for the depth levers. Word count alone cannot tell a
deeper report from a padded one -- a run can double its length by restating the same
finding against more sources. Density can: it only rises if the report brings in
genuinely new works.

WORKS, NOT BIBLIOGRAPHY ENTRIES. The bibliography over-counts: one paper occupies several
entries when the corpus holds mirror rows of it (Brynjolfsson/Li/Raymond took SIX slots in
rank7). Reporting entries as "distinct sources" is how a 73-work report gets sold as a
107-source one. Collapse mirrors first, three ways, in order:
  1. same_work_groups from the corpus (authoritative -- the corpus already knows).
  2. DOI, when present (authoritative across mirrors).
  3. normalized title (the fallback: lowercased, stripped of punctuation and an "(also
     mirrored)" suffix, truncated -- catches mirrors the corpus never grouped).
Only entries actually CITED in the prose count. An uncited bibliography entry is not a
source the report used; counting it would re-inflate exactly the number this exists to
deflate.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"\[(\d+)\]")
_PUNCT_RE = re.compile(r"[^\w\s]")
_WS_RE = re.compile(r"\s+")
_MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE = re.compile(r"\(also mirrored\)", re.IGNORECASE)
# The reference list is part of report.md. Everything from this heading on is NOT prose.
_REFS_HEADING_RE = re.compile(r"^#{1,3}\s+(references|bibliography)\s*$", re.IGNORECASE | re.MULTILINE)
# Scrape prefixes a search engine prepends to a mirrored copy of a paper ("[PDF] Automation ...").
_SCRAPE_PREFIX_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*\[(pdf|html|doc|epub)\]\s*", re.IGNORECASE)
# DRB task 72 instructs: cite only high-quality, English-language JOURNAL articles. Tier compliance
# is therefore an instruction-following metric, not a nicety -- RACE grades it.
_QUALITY_TIERS = frozenset({"T1", "T2", "T3"})


def _norm_title(title: str) -> str:
    """Normalize a title so a work's MIRRORS collapse onto it.

    ``[PDF]``/``[HTML]`` scrape prefixes must go BEFORE punctuation stripping: otherwise
    "[PDF] Automation and New Tasks" keeps a leading "pdf" token and never matches the T1
    journal entry it is a mirror of -- which is precisely how a T4 preprint scrape got counted
    as a work distinct from the paper it copies.
    """
    t = _MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE.sub(" ", str(title or ""))
    t = _SCRAPE_PREFIX_RE.sub(" ", t).lower()
    t = _PUNCT_RE.sub(" ", t)
    t = _WS_RE.sub(" ", t).strip()
    return t[:70]


def _entries(bib: object) -> list[dict]:
    if isinstance(bib, list):
        return [e for e in bib if isinstance(e, dict)]
    if isinstance(bib, dict):
        for key in ("entries", "bibliography", "items"):
            v = bib.get(key)
            if isinstance(v, list):
                return [e for e in v if isinstance(e, dict)]
    return []


def _work_key(entry: dict, work_of: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    """Collapse an entry to the WORK it represents.

    The title lives under ``source_title``. The first cut of this read ``title`` -- a key that
    does not exist on these entries -- so every title normalized to "" and the fallback keyed
    each entry by its own ev_id: NO mirror was ever collapsed. It reported 94 works where there
    are 68, and it inflated the long arms most (they cite more mirrors), i.e. it flattered
    exactly the arm being sold. Order matters: same_work_groups covers only ~16 of ~97 cited
    entries, and DOI is absent on the mirrors -- the T4 "[PDF] ..." scrape of a T1 journal paper
    carries neither. Normalized title is what actually does the work here, so it must be tried.
    """
    ev = str(entry.get("evidence_id") or entry.get("ev_id") or "")
    if ev in work_of:
        return work_of[ev]
    doi = str(entry.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
    if doi:
        return f"doi:{doi}"
    title = _norm_title(entry.get("source_title") or entry.get("title") or "")
    if title:
        return f"t:{title}"
    return f"ev:{ev}"


def _same_work_map(corpus_path: Path) -> dict[str, str]:
    """ev_id -> work key, from the corpus's own same_work_groups.

    The groups arrive as DICTS with ``member_evidence_ids`` -- not bare id lists. A version
    of this that assumed bare lists would match nothing and silently report zero mirrors,
    which reads identical to a corpus that genuinely has none.
    """
    if not corpus_path.exists():
        return {}
    corpus = json.loads(corpus_path.read_text())
    groups = corpus.get("same_work_groups") if isinstance(corpus, dict) else None
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    for g in groups or []:
        if not isinstance(g, dict):
            continue
        members = g.get("member_evidence_ids") or []
        if not members:
            continue
        key = g.get("same_work_id") or g.get("canonical_index") or members[0]
        for m in members:
            out[str(m)] = f"w:{key}"
    return out


def density(run_dir: Path, corpus_path: Path) -> dict:
    report = (run_dir / "report.md").read_text()
    bib = json.loads((run_dir / "bibliography.json").read_text())
    entries = _entries(bib)
    work_of = _same_work_map(corpus_path)

    # BODY ONLY. report.md ends with a numbered "## References" list, so a marker regex over
    # the whole file re-reads the bibliography as prose and reports EVERY entry as cited --
    # cited==bib for every arm, which is the tell. Both the citation count and the word count
    # must exclude the reference list, or density is measured against the wrong denominator.
    body = _REFS_HEADING_RE.split(report)[0]

    # Prose cites by NUMERIC MARKER ([12]), never by ev_id -- an ev_\d+ regex over report.md
    # matches nothing and silently reports zero. Markers are 1-indexed into the bibliography.
    cited_markers = {int(m) for m in _MARKER_RE.findall(body)}
    cited_entries = [e for i, e in enumerate(entries, start=1) if i in cited_markers]

    words = len(body.split())
    works = {_work_key(e, work_of) for e in cited_entries}
    per_1k = 1000.0 * len(works) / words if words else 0.0
    quality = [e for e in cited_entries if str(e.get("tier") or "").upper() in _QUALITY_TIERS]
    return {
        "run": run_dir.name,
        "words": words,
        "bib_entries": len(entries),
        "cited_entries": len(cited_entries),
        "distinct_works": len(works),
        "works_per_1k_words": round(per_1k, 2),
        "entry_inflation": round(len(cited_entries) / len(works), 2) if works else 0.0,
        "quality_tier_pct": round(100.0 * len(quality) / len(cited_entries), 1) if cited_entries else 0.0,
    }


def main() -> int:
    corpus = Path(sys.argv[1])
    rows = [density(Path(d), corpus) for d in sys.argv[2:]]
    hdr = f'{"run":26}{"words":>7}{"bib":>5}{"cited":>7}{"works":>7}{"works/1k":>10}{"inflation":>11}'
    print(hdr)
    print("-" * len(hdr))
    for r in rows:
        print(
            f'{r["run"]:26}{r["words"]:>7}{r["bib_entries"]:>5}{r["cited_entries"]:>7}'
            f'{r["distinct_works"]:>7}{r["works_per_1k_words"]:>10}{r["entry_inflation"]:>10}x'
        )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

content.text:
Now I can see the real failure, and it's an **ordering bug**: DOI is tried *before* title, so a work **with** a DOI never collapses with its DOI-less mirror.

- `[1]` keys as `doi:10.1257/jep.33.2.3`; `[13]` (the same paper's IZA PDF) keys as `t:automation and new tasks...` → **counted as 2 works**
- `[42]` "Generative AI at Work" (no DOI) vs `[78]` "[2304.11771] Generative AI at Work - arXiv" (arXiv DOI) → **counted as 2 works**

And the data itself truncates titles (`"...Displaces and ..."`), so fixed-length truncation misaligns. No single key works. The fix is **union-find**: merge entries when *either* the DOI matches *or* the normalized title-prefix matches.

def _work_key(entry: dict, work_of: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    """Collapse an entry to the WORK it represents.

    The title lives under ``source_title``. The first cut of this read ``title`` -- a key that
    does not exist on these entries -- so every title normalized to "" and the fallback keyed
    each entry by its own ev_id: NO mirror was ever collapsed. It reported 94 works where there
    are 68, and it inflated the long arms most (they cite more mirrors), i.e. it flattered
    exactly the arm being sold. Order matters: same_work_groups covers only ~16 of ~97 cited
    entries, and DOI is absent on the mirrors -- the T4 "[PDF] ..." scrape of a T1 journal paper
    carries neither. Normalized title is what actually does the work here, so it must be tried.
    """
    ev = str(entry.get("evidence_id") or entry.get("ev_id") or "")
    if ev in work_of:
        return work_of[ev]
    doi = str(entry.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
    if doi:
        return f"doi:{doi}"
    title = _norm_title(entry.get("source_title") or entry.get("title") or "")
    if title:
        return f"t:{title}"
    return f"ev:{ev}"
input.new_string:
def _count_works(entries: list[dict], work_of: dict[str, str]) -> int:
    """Number of distinct WORKS among cited entries, collapsing mirrors by UNION-FIND.

    A priority chain (same_work_group -> DOI -> title) CANNOT do this, and two earlier cuts of
    this function proved it by reporting 94 works where there are ~68:

      * the title lives under ``source_title``; reading ``title`` (absent) made every title ""
        and keyed each entry by its own ev_id -- collapsing nothing;
      * with that fixed, DOI-before-title still failed, because a work WITH a doi never meets
        its doi-LESS mirror: [1] Acemoglu-Restrepo keys as doi:10.1257/jep.33.2.3 while [13],
        the IZA "[PDF]" scrape of the same paper, keys by title. Same for "Generative AI at
        Work" [42] vs its arXiv mirror [78].

    No single key identifies a work, because the mirrors disagree on WHICH field they carry.
    So merge on ANY agreement: same_work_group, or DOI, or normalized title prefix. Title
    prefix (not full title) because the corpus stores truncated titles ("... Displaces and
    ..."), so a full-string match misaligns a mirror against its own paper.

    Inflating this number flatters the LONG arms specifically -- they cite more mirrors -- i.e.
    exactly the arms being sold. It is the metric the depth claims are graded on, so it gets
    union-find rather than a convenient key.
    """
    parent: dict[str, str] = {}

    def find(x: str) -> str:
        parent.setdefault(x, x)
        while parent[x] != x:
            parent[x] = parent[parent[x]]
            x = parent[x]
        return x

    def union(a: str, b: str) -> None:
        ra, rb = find(a), find(b)
        if ra != rb:
            parent[ra] = rb

    for e in entries:
        ev = str(e.get("evidence_id") or e.get("ev_id") or "")
        node = f"ev:{ev}"
        find(node)
        if ev in work_of:
            union(node, work_of[ev])
        doi = str(e.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
        if doi:
            union(node, f"doi:{doi}")
        title = _norm_title(e.get("source_title") or e.get("title") or "")
        if len(title) >= _TITLE_PREFIX:
            union(node, f"t:{title[:_TITLE_PREFIX]}")
        elif title:
            union(node, f"t:{title}")

    return len({find(f'ev:{e.get("evidence_id") or e.get("ev_id") or ""}') for e in entries})

toolUseResult.newString:
def _count_works(entries: list[dict], work_of: dict[str, str]) -> int:
    """Number of distinct WORKS among cited entries, collapsing mirrors by UNION-FIND.

    A priority chain (same_work_group -> DOI -> title) CANNOT do this, and two earlier cuts of
    this function proved it by reporting 94 works where there are ~68:

      * the title lives under ``source_title``; reading ``title`` (absent) made every title ""
        and keyed each entry by its own ev_id -- collapsing nothing;
      * with that fixed, DOI-before-title still failed, because a work WITH a doi never meets
        its doi-LESS mirror: [1] Acemoglu-Restrepo keys as doi:10.1257/jep.33.2.3 while [13],
        the IZA "[PDF]" scrape of the same paper, keys by title. Same for "Generative AI at
        Work" [42] vs its arXiv mirror [78].

    No single key identifies a work, because the mirrors disagree on WHICH field they carry.
    So merge on ANY agreement: same_work_group, or DOI, or normalized title prefix. Title
    prefix (not full title) because the corpus stores truncated titles ("... Displaces and
    ..."), so a full-string match misaligns a mirror against its own paper.

    Inflating this number flatters the LONG arms specifically -- they cite more mirrors -- i.e.
    exactly the arms being sold. It is the metric the depth claims are graded on, so it gets
    union-find rather than a convenient key.
    """
    parent: dict[str, str] = {}

    def find(x: str) -> str:
        parent.setdefault(x, x)
        while parent[x] != x:
            parent[x] = parent[parent[x]]
            x = parent[x]
        return x

    def union(a: str, b: str) -> None:
        ra, rb = find(a), find(b)
        if ra != rb:
            parent[ra] = rb

    for e in entries:
        ev = str(e.get("evidence_id") or e.get("ev_id") or "")
        node = f"ev:{ev}"
        find(node)
        if ev in work_of:
            union(node, work_of[ev])
        doi = str(e.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
        if doi:
            union(node, f"doi:{doi}")
        title = _norm_title(e.get("source_title") or e.get("title") or "")
        if len(title) >= _TITLE_PREFIX:
            union(node, f"t:{title[:_TITLE_PREFIX]}")
        elif title:
            union(node, f"t:{title}")

    return len({find(f'ev:{e.get("evidence_id") or e.get("ev_id") or ""}') for e in entries})
toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Insight-density instrument: distinct WORKS cited per 1000 words.

Fable's pre-registered falsifier for the depth levers. Word count alone cannot tell a
deeper report from a padded one -- a run can double its length by restating the same
finding against more sources. Density can: it only rises if the report brings in
genuinely new works.

WORKS, NOT BIBLIOGRAPHY ENTRIES. The bibliography over-counts: one paper occupies several
entries when the corpus holds mirror rows of it (Brynjolfsson/Li/Raymond took SIX slots in
rank7). Reporting entries as "distinct sources" is how a 73-work report gets sold as a
107-source one. Collapse mirrors first, three ways, in order:
  1. same_work_groups from the corpus (authoritative -- the corpus already knows).
  2. DOI, when present (authoritative across mirrors).
  3. normalized title (the fallback: lowercased, stripped of punctuation and an "(also
     mirrored)" suffix, truncated -- catches mirrors the corpus never grouped).
Only entries actually CITED in the prose count. An uncited bibliography entry is not a
source the report used; counting it would re-inflate exactly the number this exists to
deflate.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"\[(\d+)\]")
_PUNCT_RE = re.compile(r"[^\w\s]")
_WS_RE = re.compile(r"\s+")
_MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE = re.compile(r"\(also mirrored\)", re.IGNORECASE)
# The reference list is part of report.md. Everything from this heading on is NOT prose.
_REFS_HEADING_RE = re.compile(r"^#{1,3}\s+(references|bibliography)\s*$", re.IGNORECASE | re.MULTILINE)
# Scrape prefixes a search engine prepends to a mirrored copy of a paper ("[PDF] Automation ...").
_SCRAPE_PREFIX_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*\[(pdf|html|doc|epub)\]\s*", re.IGNORECASE)
# DRB task 72 instructs: cite only high-quality, English-language JOURNAL articles. Tier compliance
# is therefore an instruction-following metric, not a nicety -- RACE grades it.
_QUALITY_TIERS = frozenset({"T1", "T2", "T3"})


def _norm_title(title: str) -> str:
    """Normalize a title so a work's MIRRORS collapse onto it.

    ``[PDF]``/``[HTML]`` scrape prefixes must go BEFORE punctuation stripping: otherwise
    "[PDF] Automation and New Tasks" keeps a leading "pdf" token and never matches the T1
    journal entry it is a mirror of -- which is precisely how a T4 preprint scrape got counted
    as a work distinct from the paper it copies.
    """
    t = _MIRROR_SUFFIX_RE.sub(" ", str(title or ""))
    t = _SCRAPE_PREFIX_RE.sub(" ", t).lower()
    t = _PUNCT_RE.sub(" ", t)
    t = _WS_RE.sub(" ", t).strip()
    return t[:70]


def _entries(bib: object) -> list[dict]:
    if isinstance(bib, list):
        return [e for e in bib if isinstance(e, dict)]
    if isinstance(bib, dict):
        for key in ("entries", "bibliography", "items"):
            v = bib.get(key)
            if isinstance(v, list):
                return [e for e in v if isinstance(e, dict)]
    return []


def _work_key(entry: dict, work_of: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    """Collapse an entry to the WORK it represents.

    The title lives under ``source_title``. The first cut of this read ``title`` -- a key that
    does not exist on these entries -- so every title normalized to "" and the fallback keyed
    each entry by its own ev_id: NO mirror was ever collapsed. It reported 94 works where there
    are 68, and it inflated the long arms most (they cite more mirrors), i.e. it flattered
    exactly the arm being sold. Order matters: same_work_groups covers only ~16 of ~97 cited
    entries, and DOI is absent on the mirrors -- the T4 "[PDF] ..." scrape of a T1 journal paper
    carries neither. Normalized title is what actually does the work here, so it must be tried.
    """
    ev = str(entry.get("evidence_id") or entry.get("ev_id") or "")
    if ev in work_of:
        return work_of[ev]
    doi = str(entry.get("doi") or "").strip().lower()
    if doi:
        return f"doi:{doi}"
    title = _norm_title(entry.get("source_title") or entry.get("title") or "")
    if title:
        return f"t:{title}"
    return f"ev:{ev}"


def _same_work_map(corpus_path: Path) -> dict[str, str]:
    """ev_id -> work key, from the corpus's own same_work_groups.

    The groups arrive as DICTS with ``member_evidence_ids`` -- not bare id lists. A version
    of this that assumed bare lists would match nothing and silently report zero mirrors,
    which reads identical to a corpus that genuinely has none.
    """
    if not corpus_path.exists():
        return {}
    corpus = json.loads(corpus_path.read_text())
    groups = corpus.get("same_work_groups") if isinstance(corpus, dict) else None
    out: dict[str, str] = {}
    for g in groups or []:
        if not isinstance(g, dict):
            continue
        members = g.get("member_evidence_ids") or []
        if not members:
            continue
        key = g.get("same_work_id") or g.get("canonical_index") or members[0]
        for m in members:
            out[str(m)] = f"w:{key}"
    return out


def density(run_dir: Path, corpus_path: Path) -> dict:
    report = (run_dir / "report.md").read_text()
    bib = json.loads((run_dir / "bibliography.json").read_text())
    entries = _entries(bib)
    work_of = _same_work_map(corpus_path)

    # BODY ONLY. report.md ends with a numbered "## References" list, so a marker regex over
    # the whole file re-reads the bibliography as prose and reports EVERY entry as cited --
    # cited==bib for every arm, which is the tell. Both the citation count and the word count
    # must exclude the reference list, or density is measured against the wrong denominator.
    body = _REFS_HEADING_RE.split(report)[0]

    # Prose cites by NUMERIC MARKER ([12]), never by ev_id -- an ev_\d+ regex over report.md
    # matches nothing and silently reports zero. Markers are 1-indexed into the bibliography.
    cited_markers = {int(m) for m in _MARKER_RE.findall(body)}
    cited_entries = [e for i, e in enumerate(entries, start=1) if i in cited_markers]

    words = len(body.split())
    works = {_work_key(e, work_of) for e in cited_entries}
    per_1k = 1000.0 * len(works) / words if words else 0.0
    quality = [e for e in cited_entries if str(e.get("tier") or "").upper() in _QUALITY_TIERS]
    return {
        "run": run_dir.name,
        "words": words,
        "bib_entries": len(entries),
        "cited_entries": len(cited_entries),
        "distinct_works": len(works),
        "works_per_1k_words": round(per_1k, 2),
        "entry_inflation": round(len(cited_entries) / len(works), 2) if works else 0.0,
        "quality_tier_pct": round(100.0 * len(quality) / len(cited_entries), 1) if cited_entries else 0.0,
    }


def main() -> int:
    corpus = Path(sys.argv[1])
    rows = [density(Path(d), corpus) for d in sys.argv[2:]]
    hdr = (
        f'{"run":26}{"body_w":>7}{"cited":>7}{"works":>7}{"works/1k":>10}'
        f'{"inflation":>11}{"T1-T3%":>8}'
    )
    print(hdr)
    print("-" * len(hdr))
    for r in rows:
        print(
            f'{r["run"]:26}{r["words"]:>7}{r["cited_entries"]:>7}'
            f'{r["distinct_works"]:>7}{r["works_per_1k_words"]:>10}{r["entry_inflation"]:>10}x'
            f'{r["quality_tier_pct"]:>7}%'
        )
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())
